coredata.dc:title: Impact of battery ageing history on future degradation rates and capacity recovery effects in NMC 622 pouch cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Rohwer, Johannes
Marken, Frank
Didcock, Nico
Erker, Simon
Kohs, Wolfram
Grassberger, Helmut
Vagg, Christopher
coredata.dc:description:
Predicting the state of health (SoH) of Lithium-ion battery cells is essential for optimizing their utilization. Although electrochemical degradation models and data-driven approaches are promising for SoH estimation, they often rely on experiments with repeated idealized cycles and extended relaxation periods, leading to non-robust models for dynamic real-world conditions. This study presents a novel investigation on how different cycling sequences (ageing history) influence future degradation rates and SoH estimation. In a unique experimental design, four identical automotive-grade NMC pouch cells were initially subjected to different accelerated cycling conditions, followed by common protocols until the degradation curve passed its knee point. A detailed analysis was conducted on capacity evolution, swelling and apparent capacity fade. Results indicate that ageing history must be considered in SoH modelling, to achieve robustness under dynamic conditions and improve system safety. Under some conditions, the knee point onset was delayed by at least 50%, while in others, it did not occur at all. Additionally, reversible short-term capacity fade is strongly dependent on the cell’s state, with fresh cells exhibiting a drop of approximately 15% which gradually decreases to around 1% by the end of the test campaign. These findings highlight key considerations for transitioning from laboratory-based to real-world applicable SoH models. Finally swelling shows strong correlation with SoH (
R
2
= 0.97), and is therefore a valuable alternative SoH indicator, especially in online in-service estimation techniques where controlled capacity checks under repeatable conditions are not available.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Accelerated ageing methods
Apparent capacity fade
Path dependence
State of health estimation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental test setup for each pouch cell.
Example of experimental data defining a test batch, comprising a cycle life test (CLT), a 48-h rest period, and a reference performance test (RPT).

Summary of experimental test protocols. In Protocol 1, each cell was subjected to distinct operating conditions. Upon reaching 90% SoH, all cells were transitioned to common operating conditions for the remainder of testing. Variations in the initial cycling conditions under Protocol 1 significantly influence subsequent degradation, despite the subsequent conditions (Protocols 2 and 3) being common to all cells.

SoH as a function of FCE for the various CLT conditions during protocol 1 and common operating conditions for protocol 2 and 3, distinguished by their respective marker symbols, as indicated in the legend. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

SoH as a function of FCE for (a) Protocol 1, (b) Protocol 2, (c) Protocol 2 with adjusted axes emphasizing the linear trajectory, and (d) Protocol 3 during which knee points occur. Although the cells appear to degrade at similar rates during the Protocol 2 phase, the effect of their different initial cycling conditions (during Protocol 1 phase) manifests itself later during Protocol 3.
Swelling of the cells over their lifetime for the different protocols.
SoH versus the inverse of swelling over the cell lifetime for (a) Nom Cell, (b) Fast-Ch Cell, (c) High-CLT Cell, and (d) High-P Cell.
Capacity for CLT cycles and RPT cycles for (a) Nom Cell, (b) Fast-Ch Cell, (c) High-CLT Cell, and (d) High-P Cell.
Apparent capacity fade against FCE for (a) Nom Cell, (b) Fast-Ch Cell, (c) High-CLT Cell, and (d) High-P Cell.
Stress factors for the nominal (Nom) test cell.
Description of the stress factors for the test protocols for the different phases of the battery lifetime tests.

head.ce:title: Impact of battery ageing history on future degradation rates and capacity recovery effects in NMC 622 pouch cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Bath, Claverton Down, Bath, BA2 7AY, United Kingdom
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: organization=University of Bath, addressline=Claverton Down, city=Bath, postcode=BA2 7AY, country=United Kingdom

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: AVL List GmbH, Hans-List-Platz 1, Graz, 8020, Austria
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: organization=AVL List GmbH, addressline=Hans-List-Platz 1 , city=Graz, postcode=8020, country=Austria

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Predicting the state of health (SoH) of Lithium-ion battery cells is essential for optimizing their utilization. Although electrochemical degradation models and data-driven approaches are promising for SoH estimation, they often rely on experiments with repeated idealized cycles and extended relaxation periods, leading to non-robust models for dynamic real-world conditions. This study presents a novel investigation on how different cycling sequences (ageing history) influence future degradation rates and SoH estimation. In a unique experimental design, four identical automotive-grade NMC pouch cells were initially subjected to different accelerated cycling conditions, followed by common protocols until the degradation curve passed its knee point. A detailed analysis was conducted on capacity evolution, swelling and apparent capacity fade. Results indicate that ageing history must be considered in SoH modelling, to achieve robustness under dynamic conditions and improve system safety. Under some conditions, the knee point onset was delayed by at least 50%, while in others, it did not occur at all. Additionally, reversible short-term capacity fade is strongly dependent on the cell’s state, with fresh cells exhibiting a drop of approximately 15% which gradually decreases to around 1% by the end of the test campaign. These findings highlight key considerations for transitioning from laboratory-based to real-world applicable SoH models. Finally swelling shows strong correlation with SoH (
R
2
= 0.97), and is therefore a valuable alternative SoH indicator, especially in online in-service estimation techniques where controlled capacity checks under repeatable conditions are not available.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Reducing the cost and enhancing the performance of Li-ion batteries is crucial for promoting the widespread adoption of electric vehicles (EVs) [1,2] and subsequently decreasing the carbon footprint of the mobility sector [3]. However, battery degradation remains a challenge and leads to decreased capacity, increased impedance, and, consequently, reduced range and performance [4–6]. Predicting the state of health (SoH) of a battery can facilitate optimization of battery specification, sizing, and control strategies [7].

Various models for SoH estimation exist, each with their advantages and challenges. SoH estimation using semi-empirical models are typically costly, as they require large amounts of experimental data and struggle to capture the non-linear degradation behaviour [8]. Modelling the SoH by simulating degradation mechanisms such as SEI growth [9], lithium plating [10] and various other mechanisms [11–13] has shown much promise. However, much research still needs to be conducted to comprehensively model the entire range of mechanisms and their interactions [14] to effectively capture the internal state of health (SoH) and subsequently predict the end of life (EOL). This is further complicated by the electrochemical interaction with the mechanics of the cell. One of these effects can be seen in an irreversible increase in volume, known as swelling, which can be caused by formation of gases, passivating layers and morphological changes [15,16]. The mechanical stresses induced [17] during the volume expansion and contraction, results in a breakdown of the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer, exposing new negative electrode (NE) material, and leading to reformation of the SEI layer, further thickening the layer, and potentially breaking off NE active material.

Machine learning-based data-driven methods have exhibited some success using various architectures such as support vector machine (SVM) [18], gaussian process regression (GPR) [19] convolutional neural networks (CNN) [20,21], recurrent neural networks (RNN) such as Long-Short term memory (LSTM) [22] and hybrid methods. The efficacy of these models is, however, hindered by limited datasets due to the time-consuming nature of degradation testing, their interactions, and the variety of different cells [8,23,24].

Accurate test data is fundamental to these modelling methods, as the aim is to estimate the SoH of batteries under real-world conditions. Given that EV cells are typically guaranteed to last at least 8 years [25], testing over such an extended period would be impractical. To accelerate this process, SoH assessments are commonly performed using accelerated ageing methods (AAM), which increase the rate of degradation by applying intensified stress factors (SF) under controlled laboratory settings. However, translating these laboratory results to real-world conditions presents significant challenges.

One notable difference is due to the repeated cycling under intense operating conditions, which results in an apparent capacity decrease (fade) and recovery during the relaxation phase [26,27]. This reversible behaviour can be attributed to non-uniformities within the negative electrode, which arise from pressure gradients generated as the cell undergoes expansion. These gradients induce localized structural irregularities, including spatial variations in electrode porosity, thereby impeding complete lithiation of the graphite anode [26,28]. In addition, mechanical compression can also have an impact on the anode overhang effect, potentially either facilitating or hindering lateral lithium diffusion into and out of the overhang region [28]. Another mechanism contributing to the apparent capacity loss can be electrolyte-motion-induced salt inhomogeneity (EMSI). During volume expansion of the active material (charging), the electrolyte may be displaced from the anode’s porous structure into void spaces, typically located at the top or bottom of the cell. Ideally, the electrolyte returns during the discharge phase. However, under repeated high charge and discharge conditions, there is insufficient time between cycles for the electrolyte to rehomogenise, leading to salt concentration gradients across the in-plane electrode area [29–32]. These gradients lead to localized ionic conductivity losses, causing certain electrode regions to reach voltage cut-offs prematurely and thereby reducing the extractable capacity.

Nevertheless, these effects may become a challenge when applying the aforementioned models onto dynamic real-world EV operation [33] and excluding the effect of capacity recovery during relaxation could lead to an overestimation of degradation [26]. These issues present a challenge when bridging the models derived from experimental accelerated cycle data and applying them to real-world dynamic data [27,34].

An inherent assumption with models trained on repeated cycling is that the current battery state will be subjected to the same future operating conditions and degradation mechanisms [19]. This implies that historical operating conditions are inherently embedded in the model. However, this approach is unsuitable for dynamic real-world data, as it does not consider the evolving history and path-dependence of SoH [35,36].

This study demonstrates how past usage and SoH evolution influence future degradation rates and apparent capacity fade and recovery. This is accomplished through a novel dynamic accelerated cycling experiment, in which four identical NMC 622 pouch cells were each subjected to a different initial cycling protocol, followed by two common protocols applied across all cells. The study evaluates SoH evolution, including capacity loss, swelling, and apparent capacity fade and recovery over the cells’ lifetimes. The findings demonstrate that ageing history strongly affects future SOH evolution, highlighting the importance of closing the gap between laboratory experimental conditions and real-world use cases.

Experiments were conducted on four automotive grade LG Chem E61V pouch cells, details of which can be found in Table 1. The experimental setup, test protocol and post-processing are discussed in the following sub-sections.

A constant pre-load compression was applied to the pouch cells using the test rig shown in Fig. 1 to maintain a uniform internal pressure, set at the beginning of life (BoL). The cells were housed horizontally between two compression plates covering their entire surface area. The two plates were spring-loaded using a set of compression springs with a total spring constant of 260 N/mm and compressed to a pre-defined force. This force was continuously monitored in real time using four miniature ring load cells (Burster 8438-6010) located at each corner of the test rig, ensuring a uniform pressure distribution at BoL.

To ensure alignment of the compression plates during testing, the experimental setup incorporated threaded rods that passed through the displacement transducer, test rig plates, and compression springs, acting as mechanical guides, maintaining alignment between the upper and lower plates throughout the compression process. The opposing cell tabs are in contact with two spring-loaded copper terminal connectors, each with a circumference of 25 mm.

Temperature monitoring was conducted using three thermocouples, positioned at the positive terminal, negative terminal, and centre of each cell. A thermal chamber (Binder MK240) was employed to maintain the ambient temperature at the desired operating conditions throughout the experiments. Vertical displacement was measured using an inductive displacement sensor (Schreiber Messtechnik SM210.10.1.KT) located at the centre of the test rig, which tracked the change in height of the upper compression plate and, by extension, the central region of the cell. Cell cycling was performed using a Digatron MCT200 system, and all experimental data were recorded using AVL PUMA software.

The testing conditions for one batch are illustrated in Fig. 2 and are repeated until the desired end condition is achieved. Each batch consists of a set of cycle life tests (CLT), 48-h rest phase at 50% state of charge (SoC) and reference performance test (RPT). The CLT phase consists of a batch of accelerated degradation cycles at predefined conditions.

The RPT comprises three pulse tests and three capacity measurements, following protocols similar to those established by the United States Advanced Battery Consortium [37]. Three capacity tests consist of a CC-CV charge of C/3, with an upper voltage limit of 4.2 V and a current threshold of C/20. This is followed by a 30 min rest phase and a constant current discharge of C/3 until 2.8 V, followed again by a 30-min rest phase.

To investigate the effect of previous operating history on future capacity fade, different CLT conditions were applied to the cells during the initial phase of testing, designated as Protocol 1. During this phase, each cell was cycled under distinct conditions until it reached a SoH of 90%. Following this, a uniform set of cycling conditions, referred to as Protocol 2, was applied to all cells. After four months from the commencement of the test campaign (denoted as
t
1
), a third, more aggressive set of test conditions (Protocol 3) was applied to all cells. This final phase was maintained until either a knee point was observed in the cell performance or the testing campaign reached its scheduled conclusion at nine months (
t
2
), whichever occurred first.

A schematic overview of the testing sequence is presented in Fig. 3, where the transitions between the different protocols are clearly marked. Detailed descriptions of each protocol’s test conditions are provided in the following section.

The stress factors defining the test conditions for Protocol 1 of Cell 1, termed the nominal (Nom) cell, are shown in Table 2. This consists of a 1C, CC charging phase, until an upper Voltage limit of 4.1 V is reached, followed by a CV charging phase with a current threshold at C/20. A CC discharge of 1.5C is run until 2.8 V was reached. Between charge and discharge, a rest phase of 10s is observed. Each batch includes 50 CLT cycles and an RPT set. A force of 250 N was applied at each corner of the test rig, producing a uniform pressure of 29 kPa across the surface of the cell.

For the initial experimental phase (Protocol 1), one stress factor of the nominal conditions was varied for each of the other three cells, as summarized in Table 3. The second cell, denoted as “Fast-Ch”, was subjected to an increased (faster) charging rate of 1.5C. For the third cell (High-CLT), the amount of cycles for each CLT batch was increased to 150. For the fourth cell (High-P), the mechanical load was increased by applying a compressive force of 1000 N at each corner, resulting in an average pressure of 116 kPa. The first set of common protocols (Protocol 2) adhered to the Nominal conditions, while the second set of common conditions (Protocol 3) featured an increased charging rate of 1.25C and a discharging rate of 2C from the nominal test conditions. Table 3 provides a summary of these adjustments. At BoL, the cells were initially rested at 50% SoC for 48 h in a thermal chamber maintained at 35 °C, prior to the application of the desired compression force. No mechanical changes were applied thereafter, with the exception of one cell as previously described. All subsequent tests, including both CLT and RPT cycles, were conducted under the same temperature-controlled conditions of 35 °C.

The post-processing and data analysis was conducted in Python. Time-resolved data was aggregated on an full cycle equivalent (FCE) basis, normalized to each cell’s initial capacity as determined from the capacity test at the BoL. The median of the three charge capacity measurements obtained during each RPT was used to represent cell performance over its lifetime. Additional details on the capacity post-processing are presented in the supplementary information, Figure S1 and S2.

The variation between measurements was quantified using the coefficient of variation (CoV), defined as the standard deviation ( σ ) divided by the mean ( μ ), as shown in Eq. (1).
(1)
CoV
[ % ]
=
σ
μ
× 100

Swelling of the cell was quantified by subtracting the cell thickness measured at 2.8 V (
d
cell
) during testing from the initial thickness at BoL at 2.8 V (
d
fresh
), and normalizing this difference to the initial thickness. This relative swelling is expressed in Eq. (2).
(2)
Swelling
[ % ]
=
d
cell
−
d
fresh
d
fresh
× 100
During intense cycling there can be a decrease in capacity due to irreversible losses. This is also a reversible capacity recovery which is often referred to as apparent capacity fade.

A short-term, reversible capacity loss—referred to here as apparent capacity fade (
Δ
C
app
) is defined as the relative difference between the capacity of the last cycle of the preceding CLT batch (
Cap
LC-prev
), followed by a 48-h rest phase, and the first cycle of the subsequent batch (
Cap
FC
). This difference is normalized by
Cap
LC-prev
and expressed as a percentage, as shown in Eq. (3).
(3)
Δ
C
app
[ % ]
=
Cap
FC
−
Cap
LC-Prev
Cap
FC
× 100
The test campaign was run over 9 months. The next subsections will provide an analysis of the capacity drop, swelling and apparent capacity fade over the lifetime of the cells.

The initial capacity spread of the four cells resulted in a CoV of 0.22%, indicating minimal initial cell-to-cell (capacity) variability with further details provided in the supplementary information, Figure S3. While the effects of cell-to-cell variability can be significant [38], they are considered to be smaller than the dominant degradation trends observed in this study.

The RPT capacity of all cells throughout the test campaign is displayed in Fig. 4, with each cell distinguished by a different colour and each protocol marked with a unique symbol. The Fast-Ch cell has the highest degradation rate, while the other cells show similar behaviour until their knee point, after which only High-P cell continues along its linear trajectory.

The SoH progression for the individual test protocols are shown in Fig. 5. For the first protocol (Fig. 5a), the Fast-Ch cell has the highest degradation rate due to the higher charge rate of 1.5C. High C-rates are known to cause increased mechanical stress and non-uniform volume changes within the cell, potentially leading to particle cracking and loss of contact between the electrodes and binder material [39–41]. However, the number of cycles between rest phases (50 for the Nom cell and 150 for the High-CLT cell) does not appear to significantly influence the degradation rate. This may be due to internal cell effects reaching a saturation point, beyond which additional cycling does not exacerbate the degradation. This is further explored in Section 3.3.

The SoH observed over the second protocol, which is common across the cells, is shown in Fig. 5b. Setting RPT at predefined cycle intervals poses challenges in accurately estimating the point at which the absolute capacity of 0.9 SoH is achieved. Consequently, the transition to different protocols does not align precisely with the 0.9 SoH threshold, occurring either slightly before or after this specific point.

To account for different starting SoH and FCE at the beginning of the protocol, the capacity was adjusted to align with 0.9 SoH, and the FCE was reset to zero, as illustrated in Fig. 5c. The degradation rates in this phase are constant and similar for all cells, and this may lead to the false conclusion that the degradation rate is independent of previous history, otherwise known as path dependence. We suggest that constant degradation rates in this phase, such as SEI growth, are likely, and that hidden degradation mechanisms [25], such as particle cracking, which may initially be mitigated by the larger surface area of the negative electrode, only appear (dominate) from the onset of the knee point onwards.

The SoH progression of the cells during Protocol 3 reveals the role played by the different cell histories (Fig. 5d). Even though the previous protocol exhibited a constant degradation rate, the effect of the initial protocol is shown by the various knee point onsets in protocol 3 (except for the High-P cell). The Fast-Ch cell reaches its knee point earliest, while the Nom and High-P cells have a similar knee point onset, occurring approximately 54% later in their lifetimes.

This higher compression in Protocol 1 was overall beneficial in increasing the battery lifetime for this specific operating condition. An optimal initial pressure is based on the cell characteristics and rigidity of the housing [27]. Sufficient pressure ensures proper contact between cell components, but excessive pressure can cause internal stresses, leading to separator deformation and loss of lithium inventory (LLI) [42–44]. The higher initial decrease in capacity compared to the Nom and High-CLT cells during protocol one, could be attributed to recoverable EMSI effects, which can temporarily mask the true capacity of the cell. This is discussed in further detail in Section 3.3.

Overall, the result from the capacity analysis highlight the influence of a battery’s prior history on its future degradation behaviour and the significance of accounting for historical effects in the latter part of the lifetime. This may be because different degradation mechanisms manifest in later stages of the lifetime, even when the middle phase of degradation may not exhibit any apparent signs of such effects.

Addressing path dependency requires implementing and analysing a range of sequencing variations in accelerated testing protocols throughout the entire lifespan of a cell. This approach should be complemented with detailed post-mortem analyses to link these variations with specific degradation mechanisms. Furthermore, selecting appropriate modelling frameworks is essential to accurately estimate battery states based on historical usage data. Data-driven models, such as Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks [22], have shown promising potential in this area as they have the ability to retain important historical states and capture long-term dependencies. However, these models require validation through carefully designed experimental datasets. These approaches could help bridge the gap between accelerated ageing tests and the dynamic conditions encountered in real-world EV applications.

Fig. 6 shows the swelling behaviour for all cells, while Fig. 7 plots the inverse of swelling alongside the SoH, with an average
R
2
value of 0.97 across all cells. The vertical lines represent changes in operating protocols. The swelling trajectory generally tracks the capacity loss, a relationship also observed in other studies [44,45]. A similar trend is observed in the CLT cycles and discussed in the supplementary information, Figure S4. This correlation is likely due to the SEI layer formation on the negative electrode, which is accelerated under high temperatures and high depths of discharge (DoD) in these experiments [18]. However, for cells with more flexible casings, this relationship would require calibration specific to the cell’s characteristics and initial applied pressure [46]. Further studies are necessary to determine whether this correlation holds under conditions where degradation mechanisms other than SEI growth, such as lithium plating, which dominate at high charge rates and low temperatures.

The capacity of the CLT cycles and RPT cycles for the cells are shown in Fig. 8. The initial capacity decrease observed between the reference cycle and the first accelerated CLT cycle in each batch is attributed to increased current (overpotential) and associated ohmic losses. The variation in capacity measurements during the CLT cycles across different phases emphasizes the importance of reference capacities.

During the CLT batches, there is an apparent capacity fade after each consecutive CLT cycle until the rest period, after which there is a capacity recovery during the relaxation phase. The high capacity recovery suggests that substantial reorganization of non-homogeneous lithium-ion distributions has taken place during the 48hr rest phase; however, such relaxation processes can require a couple of hours for neighbouring particles [47] or up to months for EMSI effects [29] to fully recover.

The larger apparent capacity fade observed in the High-P cell is most likely due previously described EMSI and other localized non-homogeneities. During the beginning of Protocol 2, the reduction in applied pressure shows a delayed capacity recovery, which can be linked to the slower homogenization of salt distribution in the in-plane area. Example plots illustrating the pressure profiles observed during the CLT cycles are presented in the Figure S6 and S7 of the supplementary information. These plots show no measurable delay in mechanical relaxation between the two pressure levels, reinforcing the interpretation that EMSI recovery is more likely responsible for the observed behaviour.

For the first protocol for the remaining cells, the High-CLT cell displays an asymptotic behaviour for the intra-batch CLT capacity, reaching a limit in non-uniformity, potentially explaining why increasing the number of CLT cycles per batch does not further elevate the degradation rate (Fig. 5).

The evolution of the apparent capacity fade, as defined in Eq. (3), is shown as a function of FCE in Fig. 9. The sharp jump around 700 FCE to negative values is due to the onset of the final protocol. The transition to higher C-rates in this protocol increases ohmic losses, resulting in a lower capacity during the first CLT cycle relative to the preceding final cycle of the prior batch—thereby producing negative apparent capacity fade values at the transition.

Overall, the apparent capacity fade decreases by 15.1% for High-P, 11.7% for High-CLT, 11.9% for Fast-Ch and 13.12% for the Nom cell. Initially, a rapid decline in apparent capacity fade is observed across all protocols, followed by a progressive deceleration. The High-P cell has the most significant decrease in apparent capacity fade, due to the decrease in external pressure previously discussed. Additionally, the intensified cycling conditions introduced in Protocol 3 temporarily increase the apparent capacity fade, which is also influenced by the previous history, before gradually declining at a slower rate with continued cycling.

The elevated C-rates in Protocol 3 result in increased internal cell temperatures, as shown in Figure S5 of the supplementary information. This self-heating may enhance ionic and solvent mobility, potentially facilitating lateral lithium-ion redistribution and mitigating inhomogeneities. While such effects could contribute to the reduction in apparent capacity fade observed during the final stages of the test campaign, the overall trend of decreasing apparent capacity fade appears to persist throughout the entire cell lifetime.

It is important to note that the typical duty cycle of an EV is approximately 10% [27], meaning the significant effects of apparent capacity fade observed under repeated accelerated stress conditions does not directly translate to real-world usage scenarios. Moreover, as previously discussed, the use of more dynamic accelerated cycling — such as protocol changes — is essential for developing more robust SoH models that can be applied to real-world applications. In this context, careful consideration must be given to the influence of apparent capacity fade, as its behaviour varies with changing conditions over the cell’s lifetime. Consequently, a deeper understanding of this and its impact on ageing behaviour is critical for accurately interpreting results from dynamic accelerated testing and for ensuring the applicability of lab test results to real-world SoH estimation and prediction.

Four identical NMC 622 pouch cells were subjected to a novel, dynamic accelerated cycling experiment, wherein each cell underwent a unique initial cycling protocol, followed by two common protocols shared across all cells. The experimental results demonstrated that the ageing history of a cell significantly influences its future degradation behaviour. However, this influence is not necessarily discernible when evaluating only the linear portion of the SoH curve, as the impact of prior ageing may only become evident at the knee-point onset. Under certain operating conditions, the onset of the knee point was delayed by at least 54%, and in some cases, it did not occur at all within the timeframe of the test campaign.

These findings indicate that SoH estimation models relying solely on the linear degradation phase may suffer from significant errors due to neglecting the path-dependent nature of degradation. This leads to incomplete assessments of the cell’s true condition (state), especially in later stages of life, when hidden degradation mechanisms from earlier operating conditions may emerge. To improve accuracy, models must consider the ageing history and account for future operating conditions, enabling more reliable predictions of degradation trajectories and future SoH.

For online, in-service estimation of SoH, where regular full capacity checks in standardized conditions are impractical, other indicators of SoH are extremely interesting. This study found that pouch cell swelling correlates strongly with SoH (
R
2
= 0.97) and could be used as part of a non-invasive method for in-operando SoH monitoring.

Furthermore, most SoH models are trained on repetitive accelerated cycling protocols, which do not reflect the variability inherent in real-world electric vehicle operation. Such cycling conditions often induce reversible short-term capacity fade and subsequent recovery, a phenomenon shown in this study to be strongly dependent on the cell’s (ageing) state, with fresh cells exhibiting an apparent capacity drop of approximately 15%, which decreases to 1% at the end of the test campaign. These findings underscore the importance of accounting for apparent capacity fade when interpreting laboratory data, helping to bridge the gap between controlled experimental testing and the dynamic conditions experienced in practical EV applications.

Johannes Rohwer: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Frank Marken: Writing – original draft, Supervision. Nico Didcock: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Simon Erker: Resources, Methodology, Data curation. Wolfram Kohs: Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Helmut Grassberger: Resources, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Christopher Vagg: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The corresponding author would like to acknowledge the support from the EPSRC Centre for Doctoral Training in Advanced Automotive Propulsion Systems (AAPS), United Kingdom , under the project EP/S023364/1.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data on capacity, swelling, temperature, and pressure effects are available online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.est.2025.117690.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary material related to this article.
MMC S1
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Performance and cost of materials for lithium-based rechargeable automotive batteries
Nat. Energy

World Electr. Veh. J.
